messages.content:
Here is my full skills list for a revenue leadership application:

revenue management, dynamic pricing, RevPAR analysis, demand forecasting, Snowflake, SQL, Tableau, Python (pandas), rate parity auditing, OTA channel management, Amadeus, group pricing, PMS data extraction, Excel modeling, competitive rate shopping, stakeholder reporting, hotel P&L, forecast variance analysis

The role is Director of Revenue Strategy at Sablewood Resorts Group in Denver, a hybrid position overseeing pricing across 63 owned and managed leisure properties. Key requirements: 8+ years in hotel or resort revenue management including multi-property portfolio ownership, hands-on seasonal pricing work covering length-of-stay controls and group displacement, comfort writing your own SQL against a Snowflake warehouse, a track record of forecast accuracy within 5% at 30 days, people leadership over regional managers, and working knowledge of OTA channel economics and parity enforcement. Which of these skills are worth including for this application?

["revenue management", "dynamic pricing", "demand forecasting", "Snowflake", "SQL", "OTA channel management", "group pricing", "rate parity auditing", "forecast variance analysis", "hotel P&L", "stakeholder reporting"]

I'm putting together an application and want a second opinion on which skills to surface. Full list:

revenue management, dynamic pricing, RevPAR analysis, demand forecasting, Snowflake, SQL, Tableau, Python (pandas), rate parity auditing, OTA channel management, Amadeus, group pricing, PMS data extraction, Excel modeling, competitive rate shopping, stakeholder reporting, hotel P&L, forecast variance analysis

Target posting: Director of Revenue Strategy, Sablewood Resorts Group (Denver, hybrid). It's a rebuild mandate over 63 leisure properties currently priced on three different methods. They want someone who has owned a seasonal pricing strategy end to end, is comfortable writing SQL against Snowflake rather than waiting on BI, has forecast accuracy inside 5% at 30 days, has led people or can credibly step into it, and understands OTA parity enforcement. A loyalty or CRM replatform exposure is a nice-to-have since one lands next quarter. Trim the list to what actually supports this application.
["revenue management", "dynamic pricing", "RevPAR analysis", "demand forecasting", "Snowflake", "SQL", "rate parity auditing", "OTA channel management", "group pricing", "forecast variance analysis"]

Reviewing my skills against a pricing role I'm considering. Full list:

revenue management, dynamic pricing, RevPAR analysis, demand forecasting, Snowflake, SQL, Tableau, Python (pandas), rate parity auditing, OTA channel management, Amadeus, group pricing, PMS data extraction, Excel modeling, competitive rate shopping, stakeholder reporting, hotel P&L, forecast variance analysis

The posting is Senior Manager, Pricing and Analytics at Loftwren, a remote role building pricing logic for short-term rental operators. They want 6+ years in revenue management, pricing, or commercial analytics in travel, hospitality, or a marketplace; real fluency in demand forecasting including where it breaks down (compression, cannibalization, stale comp sets); Python with pandas for analysis; comfortable SQL against a modern warehouse; the ability to explain a pricing recommendation to a skeptical operator; and prior work on rate parity or distribution channel behavior. Which skills should I keep for this one?

["revenue management", "dynamic pricing", "demand forecasting", "Python (pandas)", "SQL", "rate parity auditing", "OTA channel management", "competitive rate shopping", "forecast variance analysis", "stakeholder reporting"]

Tailoring a résumé for a revenue science opening. Here's the complete skills list I have to work with:

revenue management, dynamic pricing, RevPAR analysis, demand forecasting, Snowflake, SQL, Tableau, Python (pandas), rate parity auditing, OTA channel management, Amadeus, group pricing, PMS data extraction, Excel modeling, competitive rate shopping, stakeholder reporting, hotel P&L, forecast variance analysis

It's Senior Manager, Revenue Science at Wanderfare Group, a remote role sitting between commercial and data science for a travel marketplace covering flights, hotels, and packaged trips. They want 6-10 years in revenue management, network planning, pricing, or a related commercial analytics discipline, background pricing perishable inventory in airline, hotel, cruise, or rail, demand forecasting at a level where you can defend the model and its errors, Python with pandas and numpy, comfortable SQL against large booking tables, and experience presenting pricing or capacity recommendations to executives who disagreed. Which of my skills fit this application?
["revenue management", "dynamic pricing", "demand forecasting", "Python (pandas)", "SQL", "competitive rate shopping", "forecast variance analysis", "stakeholder reporting"]

Here's my complete skills list for an ambulatory operations leadership role:

clinical operations, Epic (Ambulatory, Cadence), staffing and scheduling, patient throughput, Lean healthcare, root cause analysis, Joint Commission readiness, infection control, nurse retention programs, capacity planning, budget ownership, quality metrics (HCAHPS), policy authoring, interdisciplinary rounding, vendor negotiation, change management, RN licensure (Ohio)

The posting is Director, Ambulatory Operations at Marrow Ridge Health in Columbus, on-site, overseeing 14 outpatient sites. Key requirements: 7+ years in healthcare operations with at least three managing managers or large clinical teams, a master's degree in health administration, nursing, business, or similar, deep familiarity with Epic scheduling and template design, demonstrated throughput work such as chair time or room turnover, Joint Commission or CMS survey experience, and multi-million-dollar budget ownership with labor variance explanation to finance. Which skills should I include?
["clinical operations", "Epic (Ambulatory, Cadence)", "staffing and scheduling", "patient throughput", "Joint Commission readiness", "budget ownership", "capacity planning", "change management"]

Second pass on a résumé for a director-level ambulatory role — want to check my skill selection. Full list:

clinical operations, Epic (Ambulatory, Cadence), staffing and scheduling, patient throughput, Lean healthcare, root cause analysis, Joint Commission readiness, infection control, nurse retention programs, capacity planning, budget ownership, quality metrics (HCAHPS), policy authoring, interdisciplinary rounding, vendor negotiation, change management, RN licensure (Ohio)

Marrow Ridge Health, Director of Ambulatory Operations, Columbus, on-site, four site managers as direct reports. They're explicit about wanting someone who can argue with an analyst about Epic template build and win, has done real throughput work like provider utilization, has survey experience with the paperwork trail to prove it, owns a budget at the multi-million-dollar level, and ideally has stood up a new site or service line before. What should stay on the list for this application?
["clinical operations", "Epic (Ambulatory, Cadence)", "patient throughput", "Joint Commission readiness", "budget ownership", "policy authoring", "interdisciplinary rounding", "vendor negotiation"]

Considering a home infusion operations role and want help narrowing my skills list. Full list:

clinical operations, Epic (Ambulatory, Cadence), staffing and scheduling, patient throughput, Lean healthcare, root cause analysis, Joint Commission readiness, infection control, nurse retention programs, capacity planning, budget ownership, quality metrics (HCAHPS), policy authoring, interdisciplinary rounding, vendor negotiation, change management, RN licensure (Ohio)

The role is Operations Manager, Home Infusion at Wexley Care Partners in Columbus, hybrid, covering about 2,600 active patients and 70 field nurses across two states. They want 5 years in healthcare operations with at least two managing clinical or field staff, scheduling and capacity planning for a mobile workforce, experience rebuilding a workflow that crosses pharmacy, clinical, and intake teams, familiarity with infusion therapy or home health regulatory requirements, comfort owning productivity and quality dashboards, and a real focus on retention since field nurse turnover is their biggest cost driver. Which skills are relevant here?
["clinical operations", "staffing and scheduling", "capacity planning", "quality metrics (HCAHPS)", "nurse retention programs", "change management", "RN licensure (Ohio)"]

Weighing whether to apply to a regional quality and regulatory affairs role. Here's my full skills list:

clinical operations, Epic (Ambulatory, Cadence), staffing and scheduling, patient throughput, Lean healthcare, root cause analysis, Joint Commission readiness, infection control, nurse retention programs, capacity planning, budget ownership, quality metrics (HCAHPS), policy authoring, interdisciplinary rounding, vendor negotiation, change management, RN licensure (Ohio)

It's Senior Director, Quality and Regulatory Affairs at Verdigris Care Network in Baltimore, hybrid, consolidating regulatory work across 19 post-acute and ambulatory sites under three licensure categories. Requirements include 8 years in healthcare quality, regulatory affairs, or clinical operations with quality accountability, clinical licensure or an advanced health degree, survey and accreditation experience across more than one regulatory body such as CMS or Joint Commission, root cause analysis and corrective action methodology you can teach, policy authorship for a multi-site organization, and strong facilitation skills for chairing committees. Which of my skills belong on this application?
["clinical operations", "Joint Commission readiness", "root cause analysis", "quality metrics (HCAHPS)", "policy authoring", "RN licensure (Ohio)", "change management"]

Full skills list below — trying to figure out what to lead with for a principal engineering role:

Go, Java, Python, PostgreSQL, Kafka, event sourcing, double-entry ledger design, idempotency patterns, distributed systems, gRPC, Kubernetes, Terraform, AWS, PCI-DSS, payment reconciliation, card authorization flows, observability (Datadog), load testing, incident command, technical mentoring

The opening is Principal Engineer, Payments Platform at Alderport Financial, remote, owning the correctness story for a ledger, settlement, and reconciliation system used by roughly two hundred marketplace businesses. They want 10 years building backend systems including time in payments or banking, direct experience designing or repairing a double-entry ledger, strong Go or Java, Postgres at scale including sharding and migrations under load, event-driven experience with Kafka along with a real opinion on idempotency and exactly-once claims, and familiarity with PCI-DSS scoping decisions. Which skills should I keep?
["Go", "Java", "PostgreSQL", "Kafka", "event sourcing", "double-entry ledger design", "idempotency patterns", "distributed systems", "PCI-DSS", "payment reconciliation"]

Here's my complete skills list — deciding what fits a staff ledger engineering posting:

Go, Java, Python, PostgreSQL, Kafka, event sourcing, double-entry ledger design, idempotency patterns, distributed systems, gRPC, Kubernetes, Terraform, AWS, PCI-DSS, payment reconciliation, card authorization flows, observability (Datadog), load testing, incident command, technical mentoring

It's Staff Software Engineer, Core Ledger at Cadenza Payments in Austin, hybrid, joining a six-engineer team that owns the system of record for card and ACH transaction volume. They want 8+ years writing production backend code, direct experience with ledgers, accounting systems, or transaction processing, depth in Go, Java, or C# (they run Go and Java side by side), PostgreSQL including query tuning and large data migrations, Kubernetes and Terraform familiarity, and card authorization flow knowledge including how a reversal behaves in practice. On-call participation is expected. Which skills are worth including?
["Go", "Java", "PostgreSQL", "double-entry ledger design", "Kubernetes", "Terraform", "card authorization flows", "PCI-DSS", "payment reconciliation", "incident command"]

Thinking about applying to an engineering management role and want a skills sanity check. Full list:

Go, Java, Python, PostgreSQL, Kafka, event sourcing, double-entry ledger design, idempotency patterns, distributed systems, gRPC, Kubernetes, Terraform, AWS, PCI-DSS, payment reconciliation, card authorization flows, observability (Datadog), load testing, incident command, technical mentoring

The role is Engineering Manager, Payments at Halberd Commerce, remote, managing two teams totaling 14 engineers who build checkout and payment infrastructure. They want 8+ years engineering experience with recent depth in payments or financial systems, a strong systems background in distributed services and event streaming along with their failure modes, someone who has run and measurably improved an on-call rotation, experience with PCI-DSS or a comparable compliance regime, and familiarity with card network rules and settlement timing. Which of these skills should stay on the application?
["distributed systems", "Kafka", "event sourcing", "PCI-DSS", "card authorization flows", "incident command", "observability (Datadog)", "technical mentoring"]

Here's my full skills list for a VP of manufacturing operations application:

plant management, lean manufacturing, Six Sigma (Black Belt), OEE improvement, injection molding, IATF 16949, ISO 14001, EHS program ownership, P&L ownership, capital project justification, union relations, S&OP, kanban and pull systems, PPAP and APQP, supplier quality, root cause (8D), SAP PP/MM, workforce development

The posting is Vice President of Manufacturing Operations at Hollandale Industries in Grand Rapids, on-site, carrying four plants that supply automotive and appliance OEMs. Requirements: 15 years in manufacturing leadership including at least five running a plant of 300 people or more, multi-site accountability, an engineering degree or equivalent, Lean and Six Sigma credentials actually deployed rather than just earned, IATF 16949 environment experience, union environment experience including contract negotiation, and operating budget ownership north of $50M. Which skills should I keep for this one?
["plant management", "lean manufacturing", "Six Sigma (Black Belt)", "IATF 16949", "union relations", "P&L ownership", "capital project justification", "OEE improvement"]

Considering a plant manager opening and want help filtering my skills list. Full list:

plant management, lean manufacturing, Six Sigma (Black Belt), OEE improvement, injection molding, IATF 16949, ISO 14001, EHS program ownership, P&L ownership, capital project justification, union relations, S&OP, kanban and pull systems, PPAP and APQP, supplier quality, root cause (8D), SAP PP/MM, workforce development

It's Plant Manager at Brightfield Provisions in Holland, Michigan, on-site, running a 280-person refrigerated sauces and dressings plant that has had a schedule attainment problem. They want a decade in manufacturing with at least three years as a plant manager or senior operations leader, food manufacturing experience preferred but not required (they've hired successfully from automotive before), Lean or Six Sigma deployment at the site level, P&L ownership including labor, material, and overhead variance, union relationship experience, and capital project delivery from justification through commissioning. Which skills fit this application?
["plant management", "lean manufacturing", "Six Sigma (Black Belt)", "P&L ownership", "union relations", "capital project justification", "OEE improvement"]

Full skills list below for a capital programs director application:

capital program management, FTA grant compliance, public procurement, NEPA process, schedule and cost baselining, Primavera P6, change-order management, stakeholder and community engagement, Title VI equity analysis, intergovernmental agreements, transit planning, consultant management, board reporting, budget development, contract administration, risk registers

The role is Capital Programs Director at Ninebark Regional Water Authority in Portland, hybrid, entering an eight-year, $740M capital cycle covering treatment plant upgrades and pipeline replacement. Requirements: ten years of progressively responsible experience delivering public infrastructure capital programs, a bachelor's degree in engineering, planning, or public administration, demonstrated management of a capital portfolio valued at $200M or more, knowledge of public procurement law and alternative delivery methods, experience with federal grant compliance and its reporting obligations, and proficiency with Primavera P6. Which skills should I include?
["capital program management", "public procurement", "contract administration", "FTA grant compliance", "Primavera P6", "schedule and cost baselining", "board reporting", "risk registers"]

Weighing an aviation capital projects posting against my background. Full skills list:

capital program management, FTA grant compliance, public procurement, NEPA process, schedule and cost baselining, Primavera P6, change-order management, stakeholder and community engagement, Title VI equity analysis, intergovernmental agreements, transit planning, consultant management, board reporting, budget development, contract administration, risk registers

It's Senior Project Manager, Aviation Capital Projects at Rivergate Port and Airport Authority in Portland, on-site, managing terminal expansion and taxiway realignment work funded largely through federal aviation grants. They want a minimum of seven years managing public capital projects including at least three as the responsible project manager, federal grant-funded project experience (FAA preferred, FTA or FHWA considered comparable), knowledge of environmental review requirements under NEPA, demonstrated change-order and claims management, Primavera P6 or comparable scheduling software, and experience coordinating with utility owners and permitting agencies. Which skills should stay on the application?
["capital program management", "FTA grant compliance", "NEPA process", "change-order management", "Primavera P6", "intergovernmental agreements", "board reporting", "contract administration"]

Here's my full skills list for a retail operations analyst role:

retail operations, workforce scheduling, shrink and loss prevention, inventory accuracy, SQL, Tableau, Excel (Power Query), Google Sheets, POS analytics, replenishment planning, customer experience metrics, associate training, P&L reading, A/B testing basics, dashboard design, stakeholder communication, process documentation

The posting is Retail Operations Analyst at Bellrose Outfitters in Charlotte, hybrid, supporting labor, replenishment, and shrink decisions across 88 stores. They want three years in retail, analytics, or some combination with store experience counting heavily, SQL good enough to write your own joins and window functions against a transaction table, Tableau or a comparable BI tool including something a district manager will actually open, strong Excel ideally with Power Query, and the ability to explain a labor model to a store manager without using the word 'model'. Which skills are worth including?
["retail operations", "workforce scheduling", "SQL", "Tableau", "Excel (Power Query)", "stakeholder communication", "process documentation", "dashboard design"]

I'm applying to a distribution operations analyst opening and want to trim my skills list. Full list:

retail operations, workforce scheduling, shrink and loss prevention, inventory accuracy, SQL, Tableau, Excel (Power Query), Google Sheets, POS analytics, replenishment planning, customer experience metrics, associate training, P&L reading, A/B testing basics, dashboard design, stakeholder communication, process documentation

It's Business Analyst, Distribution Operations at Palladia Logistics in Charlotte, hybrid, building reporting for five contract warehousing sites where site leaders currently decide labor and throughput by feel. Requirements: two to five years in an analytical role from operations, retail, or supply chain, SQL for querying labor, order, and inventory tables directly, dashboard building in Power BI or Tableau, Excel modeling that survives someone else opening the file, and the ability to translate an operational question into a data question and back again. Which of my skills fit this one?
["SQL", "Tableau", "Excel (Power Query)", "inventory accuracy", "dashboard design", "process documentation", "stakeholder communication", "workforce scheduling"]

Deciding what to feature on my résumé for an operations analytics posting. Full skills list:

retail operations, workforce scheduling, shrink and loss prevention, inventory accuracy, SQL, Tableau, Excel (Power Query), Google Sheets, POS analytics, replenishment planning, customer experience metrics, associate training, P&L reading, A/B testing basics, dashboard design, stakeholder communication, process documentation

The role is Operations Analytics Analyst at Bramble and Rye Hospitality Group in Charlotte, hybrid, covering 47 restaurants across three brands plus catering. They want two or more years working with operational data in restaurant, retail, or hospitality settings, SQL for pulling transaction, labor, and inventory data directly, Tableau or Power BI with the judgment to keep a dashboard small enough to be used, Excel or Google Sheets at a level where you can build a scheduling model from scratch, and the ability to write a short, plain summary of what a number means for a busy general manager. Which skills should I keep?
["SQL", "Tableau", "Excel (Power Query)", "Google Sheets", "workforce scheduling", "dashboard design", "stakeholder communication", "process documentation"]

Full skills list below for an SVP supply chain application:

supply chain leadership, network design and optimization, distribution center operations, WMS implementation (Manhattan), transportation procurement, carrier contract negotiation, S&OP, demand planning, inventory optimization, warehouse automation, freight cost modeling, 3PL management, capital planning, labor productivity, SAP, Power BI, organizational design, executive board reporting

The posting is Senior Vice President, Supply Chain at Kettleman Foods in Memphis, hybrid, consolidating plan, make, and deliver under one leader across nine plants and seven distribution centers. Requirements: fifteen-plus years in supply chain with at least five at the vice president level, full P&L or large budget ownership around $220M in logistics and warehousing spend, having led a network design study through to a board-approved capital decision, WMS or TMS implementation experience across multiple sites, S&OP ownership where demand, supply, and finance land on one number, and organizational design experience restructuring a function. Which skills should I include?

["supply chain leadership", "network design and optimization", "WMS implementation (Manhattan)", "S&OP", "capital planning", "organizational design", "executive board reporting", "distribution center operations"]

Reviewing my skills against a VP of Operations opening. Full list:

supply chain leadership, network design and optimization, distribution center operations, WMS implementation (Manhattan), transportation procurement, carrier contract negotiation, S&OP, demand planning, inventory optimization, warehouse automation, freight cost modeling, 3PL management, capital planning, labor productivity, SAP, Power BI, organizational design, executive board reporting

It's Vice President of Operations at Ironbark Fulfillment in Memphis, hybrid, owning eight fulfillment centers and transportation for a 3PL where several large accounts are underwater on cost to serve. Requirements: twelve-plus years in distribution, fulfillment, or 3PL operations with several at the director level or higher, multi-site accountability covering at least 1,000 associates, transportation procurement and carrier contract negotiation, WMS deployment or major upgrade experience across more than one facility, the ability to build a cost-to-serve model and use it to renegotiate a customer contract, and experience with a peak season that more than doubles volume. Which skills are relevant here?

["supply chain leadership", "transportation procurement", "carrier contract negotiation", "WMS implementation (Manhattan)", "warehouse automation", "distribution center operations", "3PL management", "labor productivity"]

My current skills: product management, B2B SaaS, customer discovery, roadmap prioritization, PRD writing, A/B experimentation, Amplitude, SQL, Figma collaboration, API product design, usage-based pricing, net revenue retention, JIRA, agile delivery, competitive analysis, go-to-market partnering, technical writing, stakeholder management. I'm looking at the Director of Product, Automation role at Quillbase. It wants six or more years of B2B platform product management, ownership of a revenue-bearing area like retention or a pricing tier, real discovery work with interviews, SQL and product analytics fluency, API versioning and deprecation experience, and readiness for first-line management with executive-facing roadmap reviews. Which of my skills should I include on the application?

["product management", "B2B SaaS", "customer discovery", "roadmap prioritization", "A/B experimentation", "Amplitude", "SQL", "API product design", "usage-based pricing", "net revenue retention", "stakeholder management"]

Here is my full skills list, one for one: product management, B2B SaaS, customer discovery, roadmap prioritization, PRD writing, A/B experimentation, Amplitude, SQL, Figma collaboration, API product design, usage-based pricing, net revenue retention, JIRA, agile delivery, competitive analysis, go-to-market partnering, technical writing, stakeholder management. Sunderly has a Principal Product Manager, Platform APIs opening: seven years in product with three on developer-facing or API work, comfort reading an OpenAPI spec and arguing pagination, SQL and analytics fluency to pull your own adoption numbers, background in payments or another regulated space, and strong written communication for an async company. Help me narrow this down to what's actually worth listing.
["product management", "B2B SaaS", "customer discovery", "roadmap prioritization", "PRD writing", "SQL", "Amplitude", "API product design", "technical writing", "stakeholder management"]

Full skills list for my resume: nursing leadership, skilled nursing facility operations, CMS Five-Star quality measures, state survey management, MDS and RAI process, readmission reduction, infection prevention, wound care programs, staffing models and float pools, agency spend reduction, clinical policy development, budget ownership, PointClickCare, multi-site management, regulatory compliance (F-tags), staff development, RN licensure (Maryland). Harborlight Senior Living is hiring a Vice President of Clinical Operations across 11 skilled nursing and assisted living communities: ten years of post-acute leadership with multi-site accountability, a track record moving a facility's CMS Five-Star rating, survey management with plan-of-correction authorship, MDS accuracy, budget accountability with agency spend reduction, and PointClickCare reporting. Which skills belong in the application?

["nursing leadership", "skilled nursing facility operations", "CMS Five-Star quality measures", "state survey management", "MDS and RAI process", "readmission reduction", "staffing models and float pools", "agency spend reduction", "budget ownership", "PointClickCare", "multi-site management", "RN licensure (Maryland)"]

Skills as they appear on my resume: nursing leadership, skilled nursing facility operations, CMS Five-Star quality measures, state survey management, MDS and RAI process, readmission reduction, infection prevention, wound care programs, staffing models and float pools, agency spend reduction, clinical policy development, budget ownership, PointClickCare, multi-site management, regulatory compliance (F-tags), staff development, RN licensure (Maryland). Blue Heron Health Alliance, a 190-bed community hospital with a rehab unit and three outpatient clinics, is hiring a Chief Nursing Officer: ten years of nursing leadership with five or more at director level, regulatory command including survey prep and plan-of-correction authorship, a demonstrated reduction in contract labor spend, nursing budget ownership at scale, and a track record building career ladders and retention programs. What should I keep for this one?

["nursing leadership", "state survey management", "staffing models and float pools", "agency spend reduction", "clinical policy development", "budget ownership", "multi-site management", "regulatory compliance (F-tags)", "staff development", "RN licensure (Maryland)"]

For reference, my skills list is: nursing leadership, skilled nursing facility operations, CMS Five-Star quality measures, state survey management, MDS and RAI process, readmission reduction, infection prevention, wound care programs, staffing models and float pools, agency spend reduction, clinical policy development, budget ownership, PointClickCare, multi-site management, regulatory compliance (F-tags), staff development, RN licensure (Maryland). Verdigris Care Network is hiring a Senior Director of Quality and Regulatory Affairs across 19 post-acute and ambulatory sites under three licensure categories: eight years in healthcare quality or regulatory affairs, clinical licensure, survey and accreditation experience across more than one regulatory body, root cause methodology you can teach, policy authorship for a multi-site organization, and strong facilitation skills to chair committees. Trim my list to what fits.

["nursing leadership", "skilled nursing facility operations", "CMS Five-Star quality measures", "state survey management", "clinical policy development", "multi-site management", "regulatory compliance (F-tags)", "staff development", "RN licensure (Maryland)"]

Complete skills list: network planning, airline scheduling, route profitability analysis, capacity modeling, slot management, fleet assignment, Python (pandas, numpy), SQL, Sabre AirVision, block-hour utilization, connection banking, IROPS recovery, DOT O&D data (DB1B), competitive capacity analysis, Excel modeling, hub bank structure, aviation regulatory filings. Windrose Air Group wants a Manager, Network Planning: five to eight years in airline network planning or route profitability analysis, DOT O&D data and competitive capacity sources, Python for optimization work since their connection-bank tooling is Python, SQL against a commercial data warehouse, slot filing experience for constrained airports, and some supervisory experience. Which of these are worth keeping?

["network planning", "airline scheduling", "route profitability analysis", "capacity modeling", "slot management", "Python (pandas, numpy)", "SQL", "Sabre AirVision", "connection banking", "DOT O&D data (DB1B)", "competitive capacity analysis", "hub bank structure"]

My skills, exactly as listed: network planning, airline scheduling, route profitability analysis, capacity modeling, slot management, fleet assignment, Python (pandas, numpy), SQL, Sabre AirVision, block-hour utilization, connection banking, IROPS recovery, DOT O&D data (DB1B), competitive capacity analysis, Excel modeling, hub bank structure, aviation regulatory filings. Northline Coach Company is hiring a Manager, Commercial Network Planning for an intercity coach network: five years in network planning, scheduling, or capacity analysis for a passenger transport operator, route profitability modeling including fixed-cost allocation, Python or R with current tooling in Python and Excel, SQL against a booking and revenue warehouse, and a case where I recommended killing a route and made it stick. What should I include?
["network planning", "airline scheduling", "route profitability analysis", "capacity modeling", "Python (pandas, numpy)", "SQL", "Excel modeling", "fleet assignment", "competitive capacity analysis"]

Skills list to work from: network planning, airline scheduling, route profitability analysis, capacity modeling, slot management, fleet assignment, Python (pandas, numpy), SQL, Sabre AirVision, block-hour utilization, connection banking, IROPS recovery, DOT O&D data (DB1B), competitive capacity analysis, Excel modeling, hub bank structure, aviation regulatory filings. Wanderfare Group is building out revenue science for its travel marketplace and hiring a Senior Manager, Revenue Science: six to ten years in revenue management, pricing, or network planning, an airline, hotel, cruise, or rail background pricing perishable inventory, demand forecasting you can defend, Python (pandas and numpy) and SQL against large booking tables, and familiarity with competitive capacity or rate-shopping data. Which skills should I put forward?

["network planning", "airline scheduling", "route profitability analysis", "capacity modeling", "Python (pandas, numpy)", "SQL", "competitive capacity analysis", "DOT O&D data (DB1B)", "Excel modeling"]

Here's every skill on my resume: consumer lending compliance, fair lending and ECOA, TILA/Reg Z, UDAAP, CFPB examination management, state licensing (NMLS), BSA/AML, complaint management, compliance monitoring and testing, policy drafting, disparate impact analysis, model risk governance, vendor oversight, regulatory change management, training program design, legal writing, SQL (basic reporting). Coppervane Financial needs a Compliance Manager, Installment Lending: six or more years in consumer financial services compliance with at least two managing people, working command of TILA/Reg Z, ECOA and UDAAP for unsecured consumer credit, examiner-facing experience, state licensing including NMLS filings and multi-state expansion, and the ability to build monitoring and testing plans from nothing. What's the right subset?

["consumer lending compliance", "fair lending and ECOA", "TILA/Reg Z", "UDAAP", "CFPB examination management", "state licensing (NMLS)", "compliance monitoring and testing", "disparate impact analysis", "policy drafting", "vendor oversight", "legal writing"]

My full skills list is: consumer lending compliance, fair lending and ECOA, TILA/Reg Z, UDAAP, CFPB examination management, state licensing (NMLS), BSA/AML, complaint management, compliance monitoring and testing, policy drafting, disparate impact analysis, model risk governance, vendor oversight, regulatory change management, training program design, legal writing, SQL (basic reporting). Halewood Bancorp, a $9B bank holding company, has a new Director, Regulatory Affairs seat: about eight years across bank or nonbank regulatory compliance at director or deputy level, ownership of a supervisory examination cycle end to end, deep familiarity with the consumer regulatory perimeter including fair lending, UDAAP, complaint management and marketing review, and the ability to write for a board audience. Which skills fit?
["consumer lending compliance", "fair lending and ECOA", "UDAAP", "CFPB examination management", "complaint management", "policy drafting", "regulatory change management", "legal writing"]

Résumé skills, complete: consumer lending compliance, fair lending and ECOA, TILA/Reg Z, UDAAP, CFPB examination management, state licensing (NMLS), BSA/AML, complaint management, compliance monitoring and testing, policy drafting, disparate impact analysis, model risk governance, vendor oversight, regulatory change management, training program design, legal writing, SQL (basic reporting). Ardent Ledger Group is hiring a Senior Manager, Model Risk Governance after moving underwriting onto machine-learned models: a background spanning credit modeling and risk governance, practical SR 11-7 style model risk management covering inventory, tiering, validation and monitoring, documentation that has survived an independent model validation, fluency in the fair lending exposure of statistical underwriting models including disparate impact, and enough Python or SQL to read a modeler's notebook. Narrow this down for me.
["consumer lending compliance", "model risk governance", "disparate impact analysis", "fair lending and ECOA", "SQL (basic reporting)", "legal writing", "policy drafting"]

Full list of my skills: quality engineering, statistical process control, Minitab, gauge R&R and MSA, 8D and root cause analysis, PPAP and APQP, AS9100, ISO 9001 internal audit, GD&T, CMM programming (PC-DMIS), first article inspection, control plans and FMEA, cost of poor quality, supplier corrective actions, CNC machining processes, metrology, Excel. Kestrel Precision Components needs a Quality Engineer, Machining covering six cells: a degree in engineering plus three to seven years in machining or precision manufacturing, hands-on SPC including setting and defending control limits, 8D discipline for real root cause work, GD&T interpretation from customer prints, AS9100 experience including internal audits, and Minitab.
